does: This paper demonstrates that Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a preference-based alignment method, effectively reduces toxicity across multiple languages when trained exclusively on English data. The authors train DPO on multilingual LLMs including mGPT-1.3B, BLOOM, Llama3, and Aya-23, showing dramatic reductions in toxic generation rates (for example, from 46.8% to 3.9% on mGPT-1.3B across 17 languages). Beyond measuring generalization, the paper uses mechanistic interpretability techniques—causal intervention and activation analysis—to uncover the dual multilinguality property of MLP layers, which allows knowledge learned from English toxicity examples to transfer to other languages. The authors further develop a bilingual sentence retrieval method that can predict which language pairs will benefit most from cross-lingual DPO transfer.
connects: This work builds on recent progress in two areas: multilingual safety and preference-based alignment methods. It extends prior research showing limited cross-lingual generalization for safety tasks by revealing that DPO exhibits substantially better zero-shot transfer than previously observed with other safety objectives. The paper connects to the growing body of work using mechanistic interpretability to understand how transformers implement multilingual reasoning, particularly studies examining how shared representations emerge across languages in transformer layers. It also engages with the broader literature on preference optimization methods like RLHF and DPO, adapting these alignment techniques to the multilingual setting where training data for all languages is often scarce. The dual multilinguality finding relates to prior observations about language-agnostic features in neural networks.
enables: This work opens several future directions for efficient multilingual AI safety. By showing that toxicity mitigation trained on a single language transfers broadly, it suggests efficient detoxification strategies for resource-constrained languages and enables practitioners to prioritize safety annotation efforts on high-resource languages. The mechanistic explanation of cross-lingual transfer through MLP dual multilinguality could guide targeted interventions for other safety properties, potentially extending beyond toxicity to hate speech, bias, or other harms. The bilingual sentence retrieval predictor provides a tool for identifying which language pairs will transfer well before expensive fine-tuning. More broadly, this work motivates deeper investigation into which safety properties generalize cross-lingually and which require language-specific training, informing the design of truly multilingual safety alignment pipelines for global LLM deployment.
